narrative_summary:
The POLE p.Ala252Val (A252V) missense change is a recurrent non‑synonymous variant that has been reported across several tumor types, including colorectal, endometrial, lung, breast, gastric and non‑small‑cell lung cancers. It is found both as a germline allele and in somatic tumor sequencing panels, but its clinical significance remains uncertain.
In clinical cohorts the variant has been described as a common polymorphism with a frequency comparable to that of the general population in colorectal cancer patients, suggesting it does not act as a driver mutation in that setting [PMC4310866]. A phase‑II trial of toripalimab identified a single germline A252V case among POLE‑mutated solid tumours but did not link the variant to treatment response or prognosis [PMC11366758]. Conversely, an analysis of cancer‑related SNPs reported that A252V was associated with reduced overall survival in lung cancer patients, implying a possible adverse effect [PMC2993855]. Studies of breast cancer risk found no significant association with the variant and a Chinese cohort classified it as benign based on clinical annotation [PMC4204770][PMC6439399]. A gastric‑cancer polymorphism study listed the variant without reporting any risk association [PMC5482622].
Functional investigations provide mixed signals. The original POLE exonuclease‑domain study noted that A252V occurs at a population‑expected frequency and therefore likely represents a benign polymorphism rather than a mutator allele [PMC4310866]. In human induced‑pluripotent stem‑cell lines the variant was present among many cancer‑related mutations but did not correlate with tumorigenicity in transplantation assays [PMC6171902]. In the NCI‑60 cell line panel the A252V missense change was significantly associated with altered sensitivity to a set of natural product compounds, indicating a measurable cellular effect, although the direction of impact on oncogenic behavior was not defined [PMC7858122].
Population data reinforce the view that A252V is a relatively common allele: its prevalence in colorectal and NSCLC cohorts mirrors that of reference databases, and it appears in multiple germline sequencing reports without enrichment in cancer cases [PMC4310866][PMC8787147]. In silico predictions are discordant: one annotation classified the variant as benign, while another reported deleterious SIFT and probably damaging PolyPhen‑2 scores, and a MutationAssessor rating of low impact [PMC6439399][PMC11312386].
Taken together, the evidence is conflicting. Several studies and population analyses support a benign, common polymorphic nature, whereas isolated clinical observations and computational predictions suggest a potential pathogenic role, particularly in lung cancer survival and drug response phenotypes. The balance of data currently leans toward A252V being a likely benign variant with uncertain or limited oncogenic impact, pending further functional validation and larger epidemiologic studies.

mentions.quoted_text: Inspired by the association of Polε exonuclease domain mutations with intestinal cancer in mice (2) and humans (5, 8, 9), we began this study by analyzing this region of Polε in 52 sporadic CRC patients treated at UNMC. The age distribution of the patients and the anatomical site of the tumor are listed in Table 1. Ten of the 52 tumors carried a common variant allele producing an **Ala252Val** change in Polε (nine in heterozygous and one in homozygous state; Table 1). The variant allele frequency did not differ significantly (p=0.11; Chi-square) from that reported for the general population (16). One tumor carried an Arg259His variant, also observed previously in the general population (allele frequency 0.5-1%). Remarkably, one tumor (T-04-364) had a heterozygous mutation leading to a P286R substitution in the ExoI motif (Table 1; Fig. 1). This change was not present in the publicly available databases of common polymorphisms, but was reported previously as a somatic mutation in 16 other sporadic cancer cases (6, 7, 9) and as a potential cancer-specific variant in a colon carcinoma cell line HCC2998 (10). Other mutations affecting Pro286 in Polε or a homologous Pro327 in Polδ were also observed in CRC patients (5, 8). The P286R change found in tumor T-04-364 was not present in normal cells of the patient (Fig. 1A), indicating that it was a somatic mutation. The recurrent emergence of Pro286 alterations is in sharp contrast to the apparently random incidence in tumors of other changes in the exonuclease domain of Polε, most of which were only observed once (5-7, 9).

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports that the POLE A252V variant occurs at a frequency comparable to the general population in colorectal tumors, suggesting it is likely a benign polymorphism rather than a cancer‑driving mutation.
article_mentions.title: A common cancer-associated DNA polymerase ε mutation causes an exceptionally strong mutator phenotype, indicating fidelity defects distinct from loss of proofreading

mentions.quoted_text: POLE/POLD1 variants are detailed in Table 2 and Fig. 3. Of all 15 patients, ten had POLE mutation (one **A252V** variant was germline), four had POLD1 mutation (one R682Q variant was germline), and one had malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor with both POLE and POLD1 mutations. Most patients had one POLE or POLD1 mutation, while three had more than one mutation: one with S297F and S1906Y POLE mutations, one with P286R and F1907L POLE mutations, one with L523-L526del POLD1 mutation, I2070-K2072del and T2273-E2275del POLE mutations. Among three patients with more than one mutation, two had an objective response, and one did not undergo a CT scan after one dose of toripalimab.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The phase II trial shows overall responses to toripalimab in POLE/POLD1‑mutated solid tumours, but provides no specific efficacy or pathogenicity interpretation for the POLE A252V variant, which was observed in a single germline case only.
article_mentions.title: A phase II clinical trial of toripalimab in advanced solid tumors with polymerase epsilon/polymerase delta (POLE/POLD1) mutation

mentions.quoted_text: There are relatively few studies examining the cancer risk associated with non-synonymous SNPS in POLD1 or POLE. POLE E2140K has been associated with an increased risk of breast cancer [207], but not cervical cancer [208]; **A252V** is associated with decreased overall survival in lung cancer patients [209]; and N1396S is not associated with meningioma risk [210]. The POLD1 R119H SNP has been reported in multiple genome-wide SNP studies, and no association was detected with meningioma, bladder or breast cancer risk [210–213]. Surprisingly, most studies looking for associations with increased cancer risk have studied POLD1 or POLE intronic or synonymous coding SNPs that are unlikely to affect polymerase expression or fidelity.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports that the POLE A252V variant is associated with reduced overall survival in lung cancer, implying a potentially pathogenic role.
article_mentions.title: DNA Replication Fidelity and Cancer

article_mentions.title: Utility of a custom designed next generation DNA sequencing gene panel to molecularly classify endometrial cancers according to The Cancer Genome Atlas subgroups

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study reports no significant association between the POLE A252V variant and breast cancer risk.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The paper compares single, local, and multi‑regional tumor sampling strategies and recommends high‑coverage sequencing of a single run as the most practical clinical approach, without providing specific insights on the POLE A252V variant.
article_mentions.title: Currently favored sampling practices for tumor sequencing can produce optimal results in the clinical setting
